coredata.dc:title: The effects of Zn/Cd ratio and GQDs doping on the photoelectric performance of ZnxCd1-xSe
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Jiong
Wu, Yuncui
Lei, Yun
Du, Peng
Li, Can
Du, Beibei
Wang, Yongqin
Luo, Linhui
Bao, Shenxu
Zou, Bingsuo
coredata.dc:description: A hydrothermal method was employed to synthesize ZnxCd1-xSe and ZnxCd1-xSe /GQDs nanocomposites. The effects of various Zn/Cd ratios and GQDs doping on the performance of ZnxCd1-xSe were studied. The material composition, crystal structure and photoelectric properties could be adjusted by altering the Zn/Cd ratio. ZnxCd1-xSe material changed from hexagonal phase CdSe to near cubic phase ZnSe with the increased of Zn/Cd ratio from 0.1:0.9 to 0.9:0.1. At the same time, ZnxCd1-xSe material reached the higher photocurrent (3.82 μA cm−2) and lower interface impedance when the Zn/Cd atomic ratio was 0.9/0.1. Compared with the pre-doped, Zn0.9Cd0.1Se/GQDs composites exhibited up to a 2-fold increase in the photocurrent density, and achieved a smaller value in the electrochemical impedance, which indicated that the introduction of GQDs significantly improved the photoelectric performance of semiconductor.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ZnxCd1-xSe nanoparticles
Ternary metal alloy
ZnxCd1-xSe/GQDs nanocomposites
Doping
Photoelectric performance

I-t analysis analysis of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se and Zn0.9Cd0.1Se/ GQDs nanocomposites
EIS analysis of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se and Zn0.9Cd0.1Se/GQDs nanocomposites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of ZnSe (a), Zn0.9Cd0.1Se (b), Zn0.5Cd0.5Se (c), Zn0.1Cd0.9Se (d), CdSe (e)nanoparticles.
UV–vis absorption spectra of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se (a), Zn0.5Cd0.5Se (b) and Zn0.1Cd0.9Se (c).
Transient photocurrent responses of ZnxCd1-xSe nanoparticles, Zn0.9Cd0.1Se (a), Zn0.5Cd0.5Se (b) and Zn0.1Cd0.9Se (c).
XRD patterns of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se (a) and Zn0.9Cd0.1Se/ GQDs (b).
TEM image of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se/ GQDs (a) and HRTEM image of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se/ GQDs (b).
Full scan XPS spectrum of the Zn0.9Cd0.1Se/ GQDs composites (a), high-resolution Zn 2p (b), Cd 3d (c), Se 3d (d), C 1 s (e), and O 1 s (f).
FT-IR spectra of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se (a) and Zn0.9Cd0.1Se /GQDs composites (b).
UV–vis absorption spectra of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se (a) and Zn0.9Cd0.1Se /GQDs nanocomposites (b).
Transient photocurrent responses of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se and Zn0.9Cd0.1Se/ GQDs composites (a) and I-V of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se and Zn0.9Cd0.1Se/ GQDs composites (b).

EIS Nyquist plots of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se and Zn0.9Cd0.1Se/ GQDs nanocomposites (a), Bode plots of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se and Zn0.9Cd0.1Se and Zn0.9Cd0.1Se/GQDs nanocomposites (b) and with or without a light source in EIS of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se and Zn0.9Cd0.1Se/GQDs nanocomposites (c).
Mott–Schottky of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se (a) and Zn0.9Cd0.1Se/ GQDs nanocomposites (b).
Photoelectron transfer diagram of ZnxCd1-xSe and Zn0.9Cd0.1Se/ GQDs nanocomposites.

head.ce:title: The effects of Zn/Cd ratio and GQDs doping on the photoelectric performance of ZnxCd1-xSe

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Resources and Environmental Engineering, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan 430070, Peoples R China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Resources and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Resources and Environmental Engineering, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan 430070, Peoples R China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: MOE Key Laboratory of New Processing Technology for Non-ferrous Metals and Materials, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Processing for Non-ferrous Metals and Featured Materials, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, Peoples R China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: MOE Key Laboratory of New Processing Technology for Non-ferrous Metals and Materials, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Processing for Non-ferrous Metals and Featured Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: MOE Key Laboratory of New Processing Technology for Non-ferrous Metals and Materials, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Processing for Non-ferrous Metals and Featured Materials, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, Peoples R China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A hydrothermal method was employed to synthesize ZnxCd1-xSe and ZnxCd1-xSe /GQDs nanocomposites. The effects of various Zn/Cd ratios and GQDs doping on the performance of ZnxCd1-xSe were studied. The material composition, crystal structure and photoelectric properties could be adjusted by altering the Zn/Cd ratio. ZnxCd1-xSe material changed from hexagonal phase CdSe to near cubic phase ZnSe with the increased of Zn/Cd ratio from 0.1:0.9 to 0.9:0.1. At the same time, ZnxCd1-xSe material reached the higher photocurrent (3.82 μA cm−2) and lower interface impedance when the Zn/Cd atomic ratio was 0.9/0.1. Compared with the pre-doped, Zn0.9Cd0.1Se/GQDs composites exhibited up to a 2-fold increase in the photocurrent density, and achieved a smaller value in the electrochemical impedance, which indicated that the introduction of GQDs significantly improved the photoelectric performance of semiconductor.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In this age of nanotechnology, multicomponent nanocomposites had attracted considerable attention due to their unique characteristics, such as high strength, high thermal stability and high ductility, adjustable band gap, adjustable performance and so on. Binary and ternary semiconductors had made great strides in the past few decades [1,2]. The optical properties of binary alloy nanomaterials could be adjusted by changing the size of the particles, while those of ternary alloy nanomaterials could be adjusted by changing their composition [3,4]. Ternary alloy semiconductor nanomaterials (AxB1-xC) were becoming increasingly important in many areas of nanoengineering such as nanomaterials, nanobiology and nanoelectronics, because of their ability to continuously adjust their physical and optical properties through gradual change in composition [5]. The properties of these materials also make them viable candidates for biology [6,7], light-emitting diodes (LEDs) [8–11], lasers [12,13] and solar cells [14,15]. ZnxCd1-xSe, as a ternary alloy of II-VI group, is a direct bandgap semiconductor. The lattice parameters gradually decreased with the Zn atomic ratio x varying from 0 to 1, and the optical band gap ranged from 1.75 eV to 2.7 eV [16]. Therefore, their tunable optical properties were valuable for lasers, various optoelectronic devices, solar cell and visible light-emitting diodes. Li et al. have prepared ZnxCd1-xSe @ ZnO core–shell nanowire array for use in PEC solar hydrogen production and solar cells [17]. Kang et al. electrodeposited ZnxCd1-xSe nanocrystals on the surface of TiO2 nanotube arrays to form Zn0.8Cd0.2Se/TiO2 electrode with the high response [5]. Although ZnxCd1-xSe nanocomposites had good stability and performances, there were still problems such as easy aggregation and rapid carrier recombination, which hindered the effective use of ternary semiconductors [18].

Doping ternary nanomaterials with appropriate dopants opened a new way for researchers to overcome the defects of the materials and improve the properties of these semiconductor materials. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were 0D graphene fractals. Because of their larger specific surface area and exposed edges than graphene, GQDs were easier to attach to those nanoscale target materials and promote the electron-transfer of nanocomposites [19–21]. Jiang ZC et al. employed a solvothermal method to prepare ZnCdS/GQDs, and the results showed that GQDs content played a vital role in promoting the photoelectric performance of ZnCdS/GQDs [22]. These unique properties make GQDs as promising dopants for semiconductor QDs, which are highly desirable for GQDs conductive carriers to prevent the aggregation and promote the charge-transfer of ZnxCd1-xSe semiconductors. Although simple ternary semiconductors or GQDs have been widely studied in recent years, reports integrating GQDs and ternary ZnxCd1-xSe are scarcely investigated.

In this article, GQDs doped ZnxCd1-xSe composites were fabricated via a hydrothermal process. The synthesized nanocomposites were detected and characterized by XRD, UV–vis, FTIR, TEM, XPS, linear sweep voltammetry, photocurrent responses and electrochemical impedance. The effects of Zn/Cd ratio on the tunable photoelectric properties of ternary ZnxCd1-xSe semiconductors were discussed, and the doping of GQDs on the performance of ZnxCd1-xSe /GQDs was investigated.

All chemicals are analytical grade for direct use. Sodium selenite (Na2SeO3), aqueous zinc acetate (Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O), aqueous cadmium acetate (Cd(CH3COO)2·2H2O), hydrazine hydrate (N2H4·H2O), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), crystalline flake graphite, sulfuric acid (H2SO4), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 wt%), sodium nitrate (NaNO3), potassium permanganate (KMnO4), nitric acid (HNO3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of ZnxCd1-xSe nanoparticles and ZnxCd1-xSe/GQDs nanocomposites

ce:section.ce:para: First, the flake graphite was treated with concentrated sulfuric acid and potassium permanganate for low temperature reaction, then the overall temperature of the solution was raised and the medium temperature reaction was carried out, and then the temperature was raised to complete the reaction at a higher temperature. After the reaction, the graphite oxide was successfully prepared by oxidation intercalation. The resulting sample was processed into multiple layers of graphene using a high-temperature reduction process. The pH of graphene solution was adjusted to 8, and the hydrotreated product was dialyzed to obtain GQDs.

0.9 mmol (0.198 g) of Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O, 0.1 mmol (0.027 g) of Cd(CH3COO)2·2H2O and 1 mmol (0.173 g) of Na2SeO3 were dissolved in 20 mL of 1 mol/L NaOH using ultrasonic treatment. Then 20 mL of hydrazine hydrate (50 % v/v) and distilled water were added and the mixture was stirred for 10 min to form solution A. Subsequently, the solution A was autoclaved at 180 °C for 4 h. After the mixture was washed several times, the final product was obtained by desiccation at 60 ℃ for 8 h. By adjusting the molar ratio of Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O and Cd(CH3COO)2·2H2O, the tunable ZnxCd1-xSe (Zn0.9Cd0.1Se, Zn0.5Cd0.5Se, Zn0.1Cd0.9Se) nanoparticles were prepared in the same hydrothermal process [23,24].

The hydrothermal process of ZnxCd1-xSe/GQDs nanocomposites was similar to that of ZnxCd1-xSe except for the presence of GQDs. 0.08 wt% GQDs was added in the solution A, and the mixture was autoclaved at 180 °C for 4 h. ZnxCd1-xSe/GQDs nanocomposites were obtained following the heating, washing and drying process.

The structural characteristics were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD, D/MAX-RB). The morphological images were observed using transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI Tecnai g2 F20). The elemental information was performed on X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo ESCALAB 250XI). The situation of functional groups was analyzed by Fourier transform Infrared spectra (FTIR, Nicolet, IS-10). The optical properties were investigated by UV–vis spectra (UV-5500). The photocurrent-time responses, electrochemical impedance and Mott-Schottky plots were recorded using an electrochemical work station (CHI650E).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploring the optimal atomic ratio of Zn/Cd in ZnxCd1-xSe

ce:section.ce:para: The crystalline structures of ZnxCd1-xSe nanoparticles (ZnSe (a), Zn0.9Cd0.1Se (b), Zn0.5Cd0.5Se (c), Zn0.1Cd0.9Se (d), CdSe (e)) were shown in Fig. 1 . In the curve of ZnSe nanoparticles, there were peaks at 27.26°, 45.32°, 53.68°, 66.12°, 72.72° and 83.58° indexed to the (111), (220), (311), (400), (331) and (422) crystal planes according to the standard PDF data (37–1463) of ZnSe cubic structure [23]. In the curves of CdSe, there were peaks at 23.93°, 25.65°, 27.20°, 42.09°, 49.94°, 66.53°, 76.92° and 82.66°indexed to the (100), (002), (101), (110), (112), (210), (300) and (302) crystal planes according to the standard PDF data (08–0459) of CdSe hexagonal structure [25]. As the value of x decreased from 0.9 to 0.1, the characteristic peaks of ZnxCd1-xSe gradually shifted to small angles, which was ascribed to the fact that Zn ions were gradually substituted by Cd ions, resulting in an increase of lattice parameters. Moreover, the curve of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se was similar to that of cubic ZnSe, while the curve of Zn0.1Cd0.9Se was similar to that of hexagonal CdSe. Obviously, with the increase of Zn atom ratio, ZnxCd1-xSe gradually changed from hexagonal structure to cubic structure since cubic ZnSe was a thermodynamically stable phase at low temperatures. The phase transition was consistent with the results previously reported [26–28].

UV–vis absorption spectra were used to investigate the optical absorption characteristics of ZnxCd1-xSe nanoparticles, UV–vis absorption spectra of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se (a), Zn0.5Cd0.5Se (b) and Zn0.1Cd0.9Se (c) were shown in Fig. 2 . The ZnxCd1-xSe nanoparticles showed an absorption peak near 660 nm [29,30], corresponding to the band edge absorption peak of ZnxCd1-xSe.Specifically, with the increase of Cd atom ratio, the absorption peak shifted from 650 nm to 681 nm. The red-shift was ascribed to the reason that the band gap energy was reduced as Zn ions were substituted by Cd ions identified from XRD. The obvious shift indicated that the band-gap of ZnxCd1-xSe nanoparticles might be changed by adjusting the Zn/Cd atomic ratios.

Fig. 3 showed the transient photocurrent responses of ZnxCd1-xSe nanoparticles (Zn0.9Cd0.1Se (a), Zn0.5Cd0.5Se (b) and Zn0.1Cd0.9Se (c)). The responses increased sharply as the light was on, and gradually dropped when the light was turned off. The photocurrent responses of ZnxCd1-xSe were adjusted by varying the Zn/Cd atomic ratios. Obviously, Zn0.9Cd0.1Se exhibited an optimal photocurrent of 3.42 μA cm−2. As Cd ions were gradually substituted by Zn ions, ZnxCd1-xSe tended to form a cubic phase, which was a thermodynamically stable phase at low temperatures, so Zn0.9Cd0.1Se presented superior photoelectric properties than ZnxCd1-xSe at other Zn/Cd atomic ratios.

Electrochemical impedance spectra were used to investigate the difference of interfacial charge transfer resistance. Electron transport rates and lifetime could be derived at the nanoscale by applying appropriate equivalent circuit diagrams (Rs, Cdl, Rw and Rct correspond to the resistance of electrolyte, associated double layer Capacitance, Warburg impedance and charge transfer resistance, respectively). EIS of ZnxCd1-xSe nanoparticles were analyzed in Fig. 4 . The semi-circular arc in the high frequency region reflected the interfacial charge-transfer resistance. Small semi-circular arc meant low interfacial resistance during electron transport. By comparing the Nyquist plots of ZnxCd1-xSe, Zn0.9Cd0.1Se presented the smallest semicircle diameter and Rct. The minimum Rct of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se was ascribed to the thermostable phase, which promoted the electrons to move faster, and led to a lower interfacial resistance.Fig. 5 .

Based on the results above, the photoelectric performance of ternary Zn0.9Cd0.1Se was superior to that of ZnxCd1-xSe at other Zn/Cd ratios. The band gap of ZnxCd1-xSe gradually decreased with the increase of Cd content, and meanwhile the structure of ZnxCd1-xSe transformed from a hexagonal phase to a cubic phase with the content of Zn increasing. The stability of hexagonal CdSe was not as good as that of cubic ZnSe, so CdSe did not show the optimal photoelectric performance. Compared with ZnSe, Zn0.9Cd0.1Se not only retained a stable structure, but also owned a reduced band gap, thus showing the best photoelectric performance. Considering tunable band gap and crystal phase, Zn0.9Cd0.1Se was chosen to incorporate with GQDs in further discussion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploring the effect of GQDs on Zn0.9Cd0.1Se
XRD analysis of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se and Zn0.9Cd0.1Se/ GQDs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: XRD patterns in Fig. 6 were used to analyze the possible structural changes of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se (a) and Zn0.9Cd0.1Se/ GQDs (b). Obviously, the peaks of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se/ GQDs appeared at 27.34°, 45.30°, 53.70°, 66.06°, 72.74° and 83.94° were similar to those of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se, indicating that the addition of GQDs had no effect on the crystal structure of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology analysis of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se/ GQDs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se/ GQDs was presented in Fig. 6. It could be seen from Fig. 6 (a) that the particles of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se/GQDs was rice-granule, and the nanocomposites were about 20 nm in length and 7–9 nm in diameter. HRTEM image (Fig. 6 (b)) exhibited the noticeable fringe of 0.32 nm indexed to the (111) plane of ZnSe, the fringe of 0.35 nm corresponding to the (111) plane of CdSe. In addition, the lattice spacing of 0.19 nm was accordant with the (104) plane of GQDs. The result showed that GQDs were successfully doped on the surface of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se.

ce:section.ce:section-title: XPS analysis of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se/ GQDs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: To examine the surface chemical composition of the Zn0.9Cd0.1Se/ GQDs nanocomposites, XPS spectra were shown in Fig. 7 . There was a full XPS spectrum in Fig. 7 (a) including Zn 2p, Cd 3d, Se 3d, O 1 s, and C 1 s derived from Zn0.9Cd0.1Se and GQDs. The spin–orbit coupling of Zn 2p at 1045.3 eV and 1022.1 eV corresponded to Zn 2p1/2 and Zn 2p3/2 in high-resolution spectra of Zn 2p (Fig. 7 (b)). The high-resolution spectra of Cd 3d (Fig. 7 (c)) were resolved into Cd 3d3/2 and Cd 3d5/2 at 412.2 eV and 405.1 eV, respectively. As seen in the high-resolution Se 3d spectra in Fig. 7 (d), two peaks were associated with 54.12 eV of Se 3d5/2 and 54.91 eV of Se 3d3/2. The C 1 s high-resolution spectra were shown in Fig. 7 (e). There were two peaks including 284.88 eV of CO and 287.34 eV of sp2 hybridized C. The high-resolution spectra of O 1 s was shown in Fig. 7 (f), and two peaks containing 531.96 eV and 533.73 eV were attributed to CO and C-OH, respectively [31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: FT-IR analysis of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se and Zn0.9Cd0.1Se/ GQDs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: FTIR spectra of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se (a) and Zn0.9Cd0.1Se /GQDs nanocomposites (b) were shown in Fig. 8 . The wavenumber around 3430 cm-1 was from the wide vibration of –OH, mainly from H2O in the air. The wavenumbers around 1626 cm-1 and 1425 cm-1 were in accordance with the vibrations of CO and C-OH, respectively [31,32]. The oxygen-containing functional groups of Fig. 8b were originated from GQDs or Zn and Cd sources, while those of Fig. 8a were probably originated from Zn and Cd sources.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–vis analysis of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se and Zn0.9Cd0.1Se/GQDs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The optical absorption properties of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se (a) and Zn0.9Cd0.1Se/GQDs nanocomposites (b) were shown in Fig. 9 . The peak at 654 nm (Fig. 9(a)) was corresponding to the band edge absorption peak of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se, which was also shown in the spectrum of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se /GQDs (Fig. 9(b)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Raman analysis of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se and Zn0.9Cd0.1Se/GQDs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The Raman spectra of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se and Zn0.9Cd0.1Se/GQDs were taken on a Raman microscopy excited by a 633 nm laser. As shown in Fig. 10 , the Raman spectra of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se/GQDs contained the D band at about 1350 cm−1 corresponding to vibrations of carbon atoms with sp3 electron configuration and the G band at about 1585 cm−1 related to the in-plane vibrations of sp2 bonded carbon atoms. The presence of D and G bands meant that the nanocomposites with GQD still had number of residual defects in the carbon basal planes during the oxidation and reduction process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: I-t analysis analysis of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se and Zn0.9Cd0.1Se/ GQDs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: When the photon energy is larger or similar to that of the semiconductor band gap, the electrons can be excited and then form a hole pair in the valence band on their way across the gap to the conduction band. Because electrons can always be in roughly the lowest energy state and remain unstable in high-energy conduction bands, some of the photoelectrons tend to combine with holes to release light or other forms, and some of the photoelectrons will be transferred to the external circuit to form the photocurrent. The transient photocurrent responses of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se and Zn0.9Cd0.1Se/GQDs nanocomposites were shown in Fig. 11 (a). The photocurrent density of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se/GQDs nanocomposites was nearly doubled than that of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se. This result indicated that photoelectron separation and transfer efficiency was obviously improved with the doping of GQDs.Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) was also investigated and represented in Fig. 11 (b). In general, the anode current response was enhanced with the increase of positive bias voltage. By comparing the result of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se and Zn0.9Cd0.1Se/GQDs, the current intensity was greatly improved after the doping of GQDs [34], which was consistent with the result of photocurrent response.

ce:section.ce:section-title: EIS analysis of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se and Zn0.9Cd0.1Se/GQDs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: EIS spectra of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se, Zn0.9Cd0.1Se /GQDs nanocomposites and the relationship of Rs, Cdl, Rw and Rct were shown in Fig. 12 (a). The arc radius of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se/GQDs was smaller than that of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se, which indicated that the resistance of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se/GQDs was smaller. Zn0.9Cd0.1Se/GQDs presented smaller interfacial resistance due to faster electron-transfer upon the doping of GQDs. By comparing EIS Nyquist plots of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se and Zn0.9Cd0.1Se/GQDs in the light and dark fields, the charge-transfer resistance of the nanocomposites in light was smaller than that in dark due to the efficient charge separation and transportation under light illumination. To further investigate the separation and transmission rate of the charges, the average lifetime of charges was characterized by Bode plots. As shown in Fig. 12 (b), the photogenerated electron lifetime (τr) of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se and Zn0.9Cd0.1Se/GQDs nanocomposites was calculated according to the formula: τr = 1/(2πfpeak), therein, fpeak was the frequency of the highest peak. Zn0.9Cd0.1Se/GQDs nanocomposites presented a lower peak frequency as compared to simple Zn0.9Cd0.1Se, indicating that the former had a higher lifetime of photogenerated electrons [35,36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mott–Schottky analysis of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se and Zn0.9Cd0.1Se/GQDs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 13 showed the Mott Schottky of plots of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se (a) and Zn0.9Cd0.1Se/GQDs with a frequency of 2000 Hz and a voltage window of −1 to 1 V. The slope of the plots was inversely proportional to the carrier density [33]. The fitting slope of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se/GQDs nanocomposite was obviously smaller than that of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se, which indicated that the carrier density of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se/GQDs was superior to that of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se. This rule was consistent with the results of transient photocurrent responses, LSV, EIS and Bode plots.

The mechanism of ZnxCd1-xSe/ GQDs was illustrated in Fig. 14 . As the Zn/Cd ratio was adjusted from 0.1:0.9 to 0.9:0.1, the band-gap was gradually widened with the increase of Zn content in the ZnxCd1-xSe/GQDs. Under light excitation, electrons were excited to the conduction band (CB) from the valence band (VB) of ZnxCd1-xSe, and holes were left in the VB. Without the doping of GQDs, photoelectrons were easily recombined with holes to decline transfer efficiency. While in the presence of GQDs, GQDs served as carriers to transfer photoelectrons to the external circuit through the FTO glass, prevent electron-hole recombination, and effectively enhance the photoelectric properties of ZnxCd1-xSe.

In conclusion, ZnxCd1-xSe with different Zn/Cd atomic ratios were prepared by a simple one-step hydrothermal method. The results showed that the ZnxCd1-xSe nanocomposites were alloy materials rather than independent ZnSe or CdSe. The band gap energy and photoelectric properties could be adjusted by changing the Zn/Cd atomic ratio. The photocurrent responses and electrochemical impedance of the Zn0.9Cd0.1Se/GQDs nanocomposites were superior to those of Zn0.9Cd0.1Se without GQDs doping. Under illumination conditions, GQDs doping promoted more photogenerated electrons to separate and transfer, and increased the carrier density and lifetime. The study provided a promising prospect for the application of GQDs in ZnxCd1-xSe/ GQDs nanocomposites.

Yuncui Wu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Yun Lei: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Peng Du: Investigation. Can Li: Visualization. Beibei Du: Visualization. Yongqin Wang: Visualization. Linhui Luo: Visualization. Bingsuo Zou: Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51204129) and open foundation of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Processing for Non-ferrous Metals and Featured Materials, Guangxi University (Grant No. 2021GXYSOF09).
